text: Combined strength training methods (two or more of high load, submaximal load, and plyometric training) produce large improvements in running performance in middle- and long-distance runners.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners

text: High load strength training (≥80% 1RM) produces moderate improvements in running performance (time trial and time to exhaustion) in middle- and long-distance runners.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners

text: Plyometric training alone does not significantly improve running performance in middle- and long-distance runners.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners

text: Strength training methods (high load, submaximal load, plyometric, or combined) do not improve VO2max, velocity at VO2max, maximum metabolic steady state, or sprint capacity in middle- and long-distance runners.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners

text: Strength training improves running performance through mechanisms other than VO2max or vVO2max, likely through improved running economy and neuromuscular factors.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners

text: Strength training programs of 6-40 weeks duration with 1-4 sessions per week are effective for improving running performance when using high loads or combined methods.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners

text: Subject characteristics (sex, age, body mass, height, VO2max, performance level, strength training experience) and intervention characteristics (weeks, sessions per week, total sessions) do not moderate the effect of strength training on running performance.
population: moderately to highly trained middle- and long-distance runners
